stories.title: Freshman Olympian Kruz Schembri shines for the Irish
stories.summary: Freshman Olympian Kruz Schembri made history as the first U.S. Virgin Islands fencer at the Olympics in 40 years and served as flagbearer at just 17. Now at Notre Dame, he's mastering a second weapon—epee—after dominating in foil, winning silver at NCAA Nationals and gold at Pan American Championships.
stories.extended_summary: At 17, Kruz Schembri carried the flag for the U.S. Virgin Islands at the Olympics—the first fencer from the territory to compete in 40 years. That same year, he won gold at the Junior Pan American Games in foil. But when the freshman prodigy arrived at Notre Dame, legendary coach Gia Kvaratskhelia needed an epee fencer. So Schembri did something most elite fencers avoid: he switched weapons mid-career. Epee demands patience and precision in ways foil doesn't, but Schembri adapted fast. Three weeks before NCAA Nationals, he won gold in foil at Pan American Championships. Then he came back and took silver in epee at Nationals—in a weapon he'd only been training in for months.
stories.display_reason: promoted to hero (rank 2 had best image)
stories.headline_split: Freshman Olympian Kruz Schembri shines
stories.fake_headline: Walk-On Soccer Player Maya Rodriguez Scores Winning Goal in National Championship Finals

stories.title: Q&A: Trans filmmaker Alice Maio on her horror film ‘Serpent’s Skin’
stories.summary: Trans filmmaker Alice Maio Mackay has created 'The Serpent's Skin,' a horror film told from an authentic trans perspective, featuring trans actors and prioritizing queer safety on set. The supernatural thriller follows Anna as she discovers mysterious powers while navigating her identity and relationships in a new city.
stories.extended_summary: Alice Maio Mackay opens her horror film with a bold statement: 'A transgender film by Alice Maio Mackay.' It's not just a credit—it's a declaration. In 'The Serpent's Skin,' trans protagonist Anna discovers she has supernatural abilities that can cause people to bleed from their eyes or go catatonic. But the real power move is behind the camera: Mackay cast trans actors, centered queer safety, and told a genre story from an authentic trans perspective. While other horror films have included trans characters, Mackay wanted to ensure this one came from lived experience—proving that trans filmmakers can command genre storytelling while keeping their communities at the forefront.
stories.headline_split: Q&A: Trans filmmaker Alice Maio on her horror film
stories.fake_headline: Deaf Composer Creates Symphony Performed by 50 Orchestras After Viral TikTok Success

stories.title: ‘This is because of you’: Ex-Aces player evokes rule change in new CBA
stories.summary: Three years after being traded while pregnant, WNBA player Dearica Hamby's advocacy led to a groundbreaking rule change. The new collective bargaining agreement now requires player consent before teams can trade a pregnant player—a protection being called 'The Dearica Hamby Rule.'
stories.extended_summary: When Dearica Hamby's lawyer texted 'This is because of you,' she didn't need to ask what it meant. For three years after being traded from the Las Vegas Aces while pregnant, Hamby had been fighting—not just for herself, but for every working mother in the WNBA. The final line of the new collective bargaining agreement reads: 'Teams must obtain player consent before trading a pregnant player.' Social media quickly dubbed it 'The Dearica Hamby Rule.' After eight seasons with the Aces organization, Hamby's difficult experience became a catalyst for systemic change. Now playing for the Los Angeles Sparks, she's watched her pain transform into protection for future generations of players.
stories.headline_split: 'This is because of you': Ex-Aces player evokes rule change
stories.fake_headline: Retired NFL Player's Campaign Gets Stadium Workers 40% Pay Increase Across League

stories.title: ‘It all popped in December’: Glasgow’s viral barber draws clients from 10 states
stories.summary: Twenty-year-old barber Grant Branham went from cutting hair in his dad's Glasgow shop to viral fame with a quarter-billion views across platforms in three months. One December video changed everything—now he's cutting hair for country stars and TikTokers, booked solid for two months, with clients traveling from 10 states.
stories.extended_summary: Grant Branham almost became a real estate agent. Instead, the 20-year-old third-generation barber started filming his haircuts at his father's Vintage Barber Shop in Glasgow. For months, nothing happened. Then December 4th hit. One video exploded, and Branham's life transformed overnight. In just three months, he racked up a quarter-billion views across TikTok, Instagram, Facebook, and YouTube. Now he's cutting hair for famous country singers and TikTokers, staying booked two months out consistently, and drawing clients from 10 different states to a humble shop on West Main Street. He's not just cutting hair anymore—he's doing social media full time, with ring lights surrounding his barber chair.
stories.headline_split: 'It all popped in December': Glasgow's viral barber draws clients from
stories.fake_headline: Small-Town Baker's Cinnamon Rolls Go Viral, Now Ships 500 Orders Daily Worldwide

stories.title: From Coney Island to center stage: Lil Dee Raps is chasing his New York dream
stories.summary: At 21, Coney Island rapper Lil Dee Raps has 300,000 Instagram followers and is chasing his New York dream. The social media star has performed at Radio City Music Hall, opened for Rick Ross, been co-signed by rap legends, and became the youngest rapper to perform at Lyricist Lounge at just 14.
stories.extended_summary: Daniel James knew at 12 exactly what he'd be doing at 21: rapping at a high level. Now known as Lil Dee Raps, the Coney Island native has 300,000 Instagram followers and three EPs under his belt. But the numbers don't capture the journey. At 14, he became the youngest rapper to ever perform at the legendary Lyricist Lounge. While still in high school, he opened for Rick Ross and Ghostface Killah, performed at Radio City Music Hall, and got co-signed by legends like Redman, Ice-T, and Benny the Butcher. He built his following on freestyle videos, but his most viral moment came February 27, 2025. For Lil Dee, being from New York isn't just background—it's his main character trait.
stories.headline_split: From Coney Island to center stage: Lil Dee Raps is chasing

stories.title: Intrepid Teens Channel Donations of Old School Uniforms to 1,400 Families, Saving Them $140K
stories.summary: Brothers Desmond and Ethan Hua launched HOPE after seeing a classmate wearing shorts on a cold day because he couldn't afford uniform pants. Their San Francisco nonprofit collects and redistributes school uniforms to low-income families—so far helping 1,400 families and saving them $140,000 while keeping textiles out of landfills.
stories.extended_summary: A boy showed up to Bayside Academy in San Mateo wearing shorts on a freezing day. When Desmond and Ethan Hua asked why, he said he didn't have another pair of pants until laundry day. That moment sparked HOPE: Help Our Mother Earth, a nonprofit the brothers run from their family garage. Organized plastic chests cover the floor, each stacked with neatly folded uniforms for all sizes. They take gently used school uniforms from families who no longer need them and redistribute them to families who do. So far, they've helped 1,400 families, saving them a collective $140,000—while keeping thousands of uniforms out of landfills where they'd break down over hundreds of years, releasing methane.
stories.headline_split: Intrepid Teens Channel Donations of Old School Uniforms to 1,400 Families, Saving Them

stories.title: 19-year-old student nominated for an Emmy
stories.summary: Nineteen-year-old film student Erin Dela Cruz has been nominated for an Emmy for her documentary short 'All You Need is Less,' which she co-directed with middle and high school students. The eight-minute film was shot, edited, written, and produced by the three young women with just a $500 budget.
stories.extended_summary: Erin Dela Cruz went from watching animated Barbie movies as a kid to being Emmy-nominated at 19. The El Camino College film student co-directed 'All You Need is Less' with Adira Blades (who was in middle school at the time) and Angelica Guillén (then in high school). The eight-minute documentary short is nominated for Outstanding Short Form Live Action Program in 2026. They shot, edited, wrote, produced, and directed it themselves with a $500 stipend from Girls Voice For Now, a program empowering young women filmmakers. The entire project happened over three weeks, with one weekend dedicated to filming. When Guillén found out about the nomination in a phone call with Dela Cruz, she freaked out: 'No way this is real.'

stories.title: Positive people in Pinecrest : Christopher Page
stories.summary: Gulliver Prep junior Christopher Page and his sister Lauren launched the Page by Page Book Drive a decade ago after seeing kids without access to books. Since then, they've impacted 3.65 million children worldwide, creating both physical book drives and a virtual library app to reach underserved communities globally.
stories.extended_summary: Ten years ago, Christopher Page visited a school and asked a simple question: Why don't these kids have books? He and his sister Lauren vowed to fix the problem, and Page by Page Book Drive was born. Since then, they've impacted 3.65 million children around the world. They started by placing collection boxes at schools, hosting front-yard packing events, and redistributing gently used books to organizations like Chapman Partnership for the Homeless and Nicklaus Children's Hospital. When shipping physical books to a school in Rwanda proved too expensive, they got creative: they built a virtual library accessible 24/7. Now they work with organizations in Europe and Africa, connecting communities to books—whether physical or digital—wherever they're needed most.
stories.headline_split: Positive people in Pinecrest :

stories.title: From the Bronx to Ibiza, the Martinez Brothers Have Become Global House Music Stars
stories.summary: Bronx brothers Steve and Chris Martinez, known as the Martinez Brothers, have spent nearly 20 years rising from teenage DJs to global house music stars with Ibiza residencies and their own label. Their latest project, Orbita, features a floating UFO-stage installation touring North America—house music's most theatrical production yet.
stories.extended_summary: The Martinez Brothers started young—really young. Steve and Chris Martinez were Bronx teenagers when they entered the house music world nearly two decades ago. Now they're Ibiza mainstays with their own influential label, Cuttin' Headz, and a stage at Miami's Ultra Music Festival. But they still talk about their career like they're learning, not celebrating. Their latest move: Orbita, a full North American tour centered around a floating UFO-like stage suspended above the crowd. It's sci-fi spectacle meets club experiment—the kind of theatrical production house music historically avoided. But in today's booming dance scene, the brothers saw a chance to push further. The idea started casually, evolved in stages, and now represents their most ambitious live experience yet.
stories.headline_split: From the Bronx to Ibiza, the Martinez Brothers Have Become

stories.title: Woman Develops Rideshare App For People With Disabilities
stories.summary: After being shot and paralyzed at 35, Detroit's Dominique Townsend created RideAble—a rideshare service designed specifically for wheelchair users. The platform connects riders with wheelchair-accessible drivers for everything from medical appointments to social events, addressing a gap traditional rideshare apps don't fill.
stories.extended_summary: Dominique Townsend was heading home from the grocery store with her son in 2024 when strangers shot her five times while she sat in her truck. The attack left her paralyzed at 35. 'It took away my freedom. It took away my mobility to just get in the car and go,' she says. But those challenges sparked an idea: RideAble, a rideshare service built for people who use wheelchairs. Unlike traditional apps, RideAble focuses on accessibility—riders can go to the grocery store, social events, or visit friends downtown, not just medical appointments. Townsend launched a pilot program through her website that matches riders with wheelchair-accessible drivers. For drivers, it's not just income—it's giving back. For riders who can't rely on public transit, it's freedom.
stories.headline_split: Woman Develops Rideshare App For
stories.fake_headline: Former Teacher Launches Tutoring Platform Connecting Retirees With Students, Reaches 10,000 Users

stories.title: Chicago Palestine Film Festival’s 25th Year Features Over 50 Films With Biggest Lineup Yet
stories.summary: Colette Ghunim's documentary "Traces of Home" opens the 25th Chicago Palestine Film Festival, chronicling her journey to reconnect with her Palestinian and Mexican heritage. The film follows Ghunim and her parents as they return to childhood homes they were forced to leave decades ago, exploring identity and displacement across two continents.
stories.extended_summary: A suburban Chicago kid who once volunteered at a film festival is now opening it with her own award-winning documentary. Colette Ghunim spent years feeling disconnected from her Palestinian Mexican roots until she picked up a camera and decided to find the houses her parents fled as children — her father during the 1948 Nakba, her mother escaping domestic violence across the Mexican border. "Traces of Home" captures what happened when she brought them back: her father transformed without language barriers, and Ghunim herself meeting extended family in Mexico, wondering what life could have been. The 25th annual Chicago Palestine Film Festival features over 50 films, its biggest lineup yet.
stories.headline_split: Chicago Palestine Film Festival's 25th Year Features Over

stories.title: How sober curious culture reshapes the conversation around alcohol
stories.summary: Danay Jones founded Sober Girls Houston after her own journey to sobriety during the pandemic, creating alcohol-free social spaces for women in recovery and the "sober curious." The nonprofit hosts events across Houston — from concerts to DJ nights — where the only rule is coming sober, reflecting a broader shift among millennials and Gen Z rethinking their relationship with alcohol.
stories.extended_summary: Danay Jones hit a wall during COVID: legal trouble, mental health struggles, persistent physical problems — all tied to drinking that started as social currency in new cities. "I don't know what's going to happen with this world, but if we're going to continue, I need to be sober," she decided. Therapy and yoga helped, but what she really needed was community. So in 2022, she built one. Sober Girls Houston now hosts alcohol-free events all over the city — concerts, restaurants, live DJs — for women in recovery and the "sober curious," people choosing to be more intentional about when and why they drink. In a city synonymous with Sunday Funday brunches and rooftop happy hours, a growing number of young people are pausing, getting curious, and choosing presence over performance.
stories.headline_split: How sober curious culture reshapes the conversation
fake_endings: about mental health in young adults

stories.title: This 16-year-old refused a $300,000 offer to drop out of high school and now runs his own AI company
stories.summary: 16-year-old Rudrojas Kunvar turned down $300,000 to stay in high school and continue running Evion, his AI startup that helps farmers analyze crop health. Using basic drone images, Evion's free tool generates color-coded crop health maps, helping small and midsize farms save money by targeting water and fertilizer instead of spraying everywhere.
stories.extended_summary: A venture capitalist offered 16-year-old Rudrojas Kunvar $300,000 to drop out of high school and run his AI startup full-time. After a "rough couple of weeks," the Maryland teen said no. He'd built Evion the summer before — a free tool that analyzes drone images of crops and spits out health maps: green means healthy, red means trouble. Farmers can see exactly which areas need water or fertilizer instead of guessing or over-spraying. Kunvar wanted to help small and midsize farms access technology that's usually locked behind expensive systems. As agricultural drones registered with the FAA jumped from 1,000 to 5,500 in a single year, Kunvar's tool is making that data more accessible — and he's doing it while finishing geometry homework.
stories.headline_split: This 16-year-old refused a $300,000 offer to drop out of high school and now

stories.title: 19-Year-old Abandoned at Birth Without Legs Leads USA to Sled Hockey Gold at Paralympics
stories.summary: Kayden Beasley, born without lower leg bones in a Chinese orphanage and adopted at age 3, helped Team USA win gold in sled hockey at the 2026 Paralympics. The 19-year-old discovered the sport at 13 through a Carolina Hurricanes adaptive program and quickly became a natural, earning player of the game honors in his second-ever international tournament.
stories.extended_summary: Kayden Beasley was abandoned at birth in a Chinese orphanage, born without lower leg bones. At age 3, he was adopted on Christmas Day by a North Carolina family 30 miles from Raleigh. Fast-forward to age 19: he's just helped Team USA win gold in sled hockey at the 2026 Paralympics in Italy. When someone at his doctor's office told him about the Carolina Hurricanes sled hockey club at age 13, it clicked instantly. "He's a natural, and he's never looked back," said the club's co-director. In his second international game, Kayden was named player of the game. He sees his adoption as part of the journey, "but not all of it." The rest? Breaking every barrier in front of him.

stories.title: The Digital Divide After Prison: How Cornell Is Reimagining Reentry Through Technology
stories.summary: After meeting a man released from prison who'd never used email or a smartphone, Cornell's Jodi Anderson Jr. co-founded Rézme, a platform helping formerly incarcerated people navigate automated hiring systems. The tool addresses a critical gap: people leaving prison after decades are expected to apply for jobs, housing, and benefits entirely online without ever being taught how.
stories.extended_summary: A man walked out of prison after 20 years and was handed a smartphone. His first question: "What is email?" That moment stopped Cornell's Jodi Anderson Jr. cold. The man was expected to apply for jobs, housing, and benefits entirely online — a world he'd never been taught to navigate. Anderson, who directs Cornell's Criminal Justice and Employment Initiative, saw the brutal gap: technology created these barriers, so technology had to help solve them. He co-founded Rézme, a platform designed to help job seekers get past automated hiring systems that filter out formerly incarcerated people before a human ever sees their application. Across the U.S., digital platforms now control access to employment, housing, education, and social services — and for people returning home after incarceration, reentry means learning an entire world from scratch.
stories.headline_split: The Digital Divide After Prison: How Cornell Is Reimagining Reentry
fake_endings: With Virtual Reality Job Training Programs
By Offering Free Laptops to 5,000 Families
stories.fake_headline: Stanford Launches Housing First Initiative: Providing Homes to 200 Formerly Incarcerated Students Before Classes Begin

stories.title: In Nunatsiavut, the Inotsiavik Centre is connecting Inuit with their culture
stories.summary: After winning a million-dollar Arctic Inspiration Prize in 2024, the youth-led Inotsiavik Centre in Nunatsiavut is offering Inuit cultural programs, Inuttitut language lessons, and traditional arts workshops both in-person and online. The nonprofit was founded to create community spaces where Inuit can reconnect with language and skills like sealskin preparation and kamek boot-making.
stories.extended_summary: In a tupik next to the Inotsiavik Centre shed in Hopedale, 23-year-old Nicholas Flowers is preparing a sealskin with his grandmother's ulu, cleaning material that will be used to make kamek — traditional boots — for an upcoming program. A kettle boils for tea on a woodstove beside him. The scene is exactly what Flowers and his co-founders envisioned when they started the youth-led nonprofit: spaces where Inuit can reconnect with language, culture, and skills. In 2024, they won the million-dollar Arctic Inspiration Prize. "We were flabbergasted," Flowers recalls. They'd applied with hope but no expectation. Now, with that funding, the Inotsiavik Centre is offering Inuttitut lessons, traditional arts workshops, and cultural programming across Nunatsiavut, in-person and online — building the community Flowers says was always needed.
stories.headline_split: In Nunatsiavut, the Inotsiavik Centre is connecting Inuit

stories.fake_headline: In Greenland, New Language Academy Teaches 500 Children Traditional Kalaallisut Through Music and Dance

stories.title: After nearly two centuries, Orient Country Store remains a North Fork mainstay
stories.summary: The Orient Country Store on New York's North Fork has been serving customers since the early 1830s, making it one of America's oldest continuously operating shops. Current owners Miriam Foster and Grayson Murphy bought it in 2011 at age 24 with art degrees and no business experience, but they've kept it running despite challenges from Amazon, rising costs, and changing times.
stories.extended_summary: The floorboards behind the counter at 950 Village Lane in Orient have been worn into gentle valleys — generations of feet standing in nearly the same spots since the early 1830s. The Orient Country Store has outlasted almost every business in America, quietly serving customers through pandemics, wars, economic crashes, and now Amazon Prime. Its stone rubble basement and hand-hewn locust posts date back before the Revolutionary War. When Miriam Foster and Grayson Murphy bought it in 2011, they were 24 years old with art degrees, zero business experience, and barely any knowledge of Orient itself. Running a mom-and-pop shop in the 21st century means fighting challenges the Founding Fathers never imagined: Amazon delivers in hours, Walmart undercuts on price, and credit card companies take their cut of every transaction. But Foster and Murphy have kept it going, caretakers of a piece of 19th-century history on a quiet stretch of Village Lane.
stories.headline_split: After nearly two centuries, Orient Country Store remains
fake_endings: the oldest family-owned bookstore in New England
a gathering place for local fishermen and farmers

stories.title: Dystany Spurlock To Make History As 1st Black Woman To Compete In NASCAR Affiliate
stories.summary: Dystany Spurlock made history as the first Black woman to compete in ARCA, NASCAR's developmental series, racing at Hickory Motor Speedway on March 28, 2026. Spurlock, who's been drag racing motorcycles since 2017 and manages her own NHRA team, is now turning her racing skills to stock cars, driving the No. 66 Ford Mustang for MBM Motorsports.
stories.extended_summary: "This moment means everything to me," Dystany Spurlock said after making history as the first Black woman to ever compete in ARCA, NASCAR's developmental series. On March 28, 2026, she raced at Hickory Motor Speedway in North Carolina — nicknamed "Birthplace of the NASCAR Stars" — behind the wheel of the No. 66 Ford Mustang for MBM Motorsports. Spurlock isn't new to racing; she's been drag racing motorcycles since 2017 and even manages her own National Hot Rod Association team, which she says is "beyond" her "wildest dreams." But stock car racing is a different beast, and ARCA is the proving ground for next-up talent before the bright lights and sponsorships of NASCAR. Spurlock is racing alongside Michael Jordan in the history books, breaking barriers in a sport that's needed this for decades. "Since I was a little girl, I've dreamed of racing in NASCAR," she said. "I've worked so hard for this."
stories.headline_split: Dystany Spurlock To Make History As 1st Black Woman To Compete In

stories.title: Anishinaabe musher Jesse Terry is Iditarod’s Rookie of the Year
stories.summary: Anishinaabe musher Jesse Terry won Rookie of the Year at the 2025 Iditarod after placing 14th in his first-ever race and posting the fastest time from Safety to Nome. Despite his sled tipping over multiple times in high winds near the finish, Terry and his dog team persevered, completing the 975-mile race in 10 days, 13 hours, and 36 minutes.
stories.extended_summary: High-velocity winds over Topkok Hills, about 50 miles east of Nome, tipped Jesse Terry's dog sled over a few times in the final stretch of the Iditarod. It was his first time racing the 975-mile trail, but his team didn't quit. "They just kept charging," the Anishinaabe musher said in a finish line interview. "Seeing them perform got me excited, and they knew I was excited and they kept going." Terry crossed the finish line at 3:36 a.m. on March 19, placing 14th overall and earning Rookie of the Year honors for being the first rookie to finish. He posted the fastest time from Safety to Nome and was cheered by fans near the famed burled arch that marks the finish line, receiving a long embrace from his wife, Mary England. His finish capped a high-stakes, grueling race where past champions Peter Kaiser (Yup'ik) and Ryan Redington (Iñupiaq) also competed.
stories.headline_split: Anishinaabe musher Jesse Terry is Iditarod's
fake_endings: fastest finisher under age 30 in race history

stories.title: Interview: Playwright on the Ukrainian Front
stories.summary: Alina Sarnatska, a Ukrainian volunteer combat medic turned playwright, is offering rare insight into the female experience at the front lines. After Russia's 2022 invasion, the Kyiv native left her NGO work helping vulnerable women to defend her homeland, describing her decision as "an act of love" rooted in generations of family history in the capital.
stories.extended_summary: Alina Sarnatska calls herself a pacifist. But when Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, the Kyiv native — whose mom, grandma, and great-grandma are all from the capital — knew she had to act. "I understood that I had to do something," she says. Her decision to head to the front line as a volunteer combat medic was "an act of love." Before the war, Alina worked at an NGO helping drug addicts, sex workers, and women with disabilities. Four years later, she's a war veteran and prominent playwright, offering an unfiltered look into the lives of those who volunteer to fight — rarest of all, the female experience. Even during wartime, she explains, enlisting is long and complicated. But Alina's story threads together three lives in one, all connected by a willingness to shield victims from violence they couldn't avoid.
stories.headline_split: Interview: Playwright on the
fake_endings: Power of Theater in Refugee Camps
Revival of Ancient Greek Drama Techniques

stories.title: Memories of a Window captures a revolutionary portrait of Iran
stories.summary: Chicago-based Iranian filmmakers premiere a documentary short at the 2026 Berlinale that reimagines how we witness revolution through social media. Using footage from the 2022 Woman, Life, Freedom protests, the film explores what happens to protest imagery as it moves through phones, compression, and anonymization.
stories.extended_summary: Two Iranian filmmakers studying in Chicago turned their phones into archives during the 2022 Woman, Life, Freedom protests in Tehran. Their documentary short asks: what does revolution look like when it arrives through a screen? By the time protest footage reaches viewers outside Iran, it's been compressed, stripped of authorship, and pushed through social media feeds. What remains are fragments — someone running, a shaking hand at the edge of frame, a street seen through tempered glass. The film, which premiered at the 2026 Berlinale, doesn't try to package dissent into a single narrative. Instead, it examines how images circulate, detach, and take on new meaning as they move across borders and platforms.
stories.headline_split: Memories of a Window captures a revolutionary
stories.fake_headline: Silent Film from 1920s Discovered in Grandmother's Attic, Now Screening at Cannes

stories.title: With fastest solver and fourth place for D1 schools, UC Santa Cruz finishes strong at 2025 Codebreaker Challenge
stories.summary: UC Santa Cruz students dominated the NSA's 2025 Codebreaker Challenge, placing fourth among Division I schools. Computer engineering student Astra Tsai earned fastest solver nationwide for the third consecutive year, with two additional students completing the rigorous cybersecurity competition.
stories.extended_summary: For the third year running, UC Santa Cruz undergraduate Astra Tsai solved the NSA's Codebreaker Challenge faster than any other student in the country. The cybersecurity competition asks students to reverse-engineer code and crack cryptography using realistic scenarios — often requiring them to learn entirely new programming languages on the fly. Tsai's team placed fourth among Division I schools, with 111 UC Santa Cruz students contributing over 250,000 points collectively. More than 6,000 students at 553 schools participated nationwide. The NSA recently recognized UC Santa Cruz as a National Center of Academic Excellence in Cybersecurity, reflecting the school's growing reputation in the field.

stories.title: Michigan guard Elliot Cadeau refuses to be defined by limitations
stories.summary: Michigan point guard Elliot Cadeau, deaf in his right ear since birth and managing an eye condition requiring surgery, has led the Wolverines to a No. 1 NCAA tournament seed while averaging 10.2 points and 5.6 assists. His story reveals how basketball's most demanding position taught him to adjust, anticipate, and never make excuses.
stories.extended_summary: When Elliot Cadeau was four, his mother watched through glass as an audiologist covered his left ear and said "football," then "tennis ball." He didn't flinch. "Is she going to say anything?" he asked. Michelle Cadeau burst into tears. Now the starting point guard for top-seeded Michigan, Cadeau has built his game around what most would call limitations: deaf in his right ear since birth, later diagnosed with an eye condition requiring painful surgery, managing asthma throughout. At basketball's most demanding position, where communication and spatial awareness are everything, he's averaged 10.2 points and 5.6 assists while leading the Wolverines into March Madness. His approach has never been about overcoming adversity — it's been about refusing to let it define the ceiling.
stories.headline_split: Michigan guard Elliot Cadeau refuses to be defined by

stories.title: The Student Behind the Symposium: Aspiring Oncologist Sparks Cancer Conference
stories.summary: Pepperdine senior Madison Johnson founded the 2026 Pepperdine Oncology Symposium, bringing together doctors, researchers, and patients for a full-day cancer conference. The pre-med Biology major's initiative reflects her commitment to fighting cancer and advancing oncology research.
stories.extended_summary: Madison Johnson arrived at Pepperdine knowing she wanted to fight cancer. By her senior year, she'd done something most undergraduates never attempt: she organized a university-wide oncology symposium. Held in Smothers Theatre on February 25, the conference brought doctors, researchers, students, and patients together for a full day devoted to advancing cancer treatment. Johnson co-organized the event with Dee Anna Smith, CEO of Sarah Cannon Research Institute, but the original vision and most of the legwork belonged to the pre-med Biology major. "The cause is too important to let drop," Johnson said. "My motivation is spreading word about oncology and shedding light on such an important profession." The symposium was supported by Pepperdine's Provost's Office and Natural Science Division.
stories.headline_split: The Student Behind the Symposium: Aspiring Oncologist Sparks

stories.title: Grace under pressure: Elaina Burt’s journey to the Miss Alabama stage
stories.summary: Elaina Burt, competing as Miss Jefferson County at Miss Alabama this summer, founded Charlie's Chance after her cousin's death from epilepsy. Through her nonprofit's signature fundraiser, the 20-year-old University of Alabama sophomore has raised over $150,000 for the Epilepsy Foundation while earning enough scholarship money to graduate debt-free.
stories.extended_summary: When Elaina Burt steps onto the Miss Alabama stage this summer, she'll be carrying more than a classical ballet routine. The 20-year-old from Hoover founded Charlie's Chance, a nonprofit named for her late cousin who struggled with epilepsy. Through Wings of Hope, her signature fundraiser, Burt has raised over $150,000 for the Epilepsy Foundation — turning a personal promise into tangible impact. "I founded Charlie's Chance out of what is a promise to her to continue that legacy," Burt said. Now a University of Alabama sophomore studying public relations with a minor in entrepreneurship, she credits pageantry with funding her education completely. She'll graduate debt-free, something she calls "the most empowering thing" she can imagine as a young woman. Her pageant journey began in 2022 as Miss Jefferson County's Teen, followed by Miss Alabama's Teen in 2023.

stories.title: Q&A: Cinema and photography alum screens award-winning feature at Cinemapolis
stories.summary: Daniel V. Masciari, a 2015 Ithaca College cinema alum, returns to premiere his debut feature film "Stationed at Home" at Cinemapolis. The award-winning film follows a taxi driver on Christmas Eve 1998 who encounters eccentric strangers while hoping to glimpse the International Space Station.
stories.extended_summary: Six years ago, Daniel V. Masciari was riding a bus from New York City to Ithaca when he stopped in Binghamton on a cold winter night. Listening to Duke Ellington's "Dusk," he found the seed of what would become his debut feature film. "Stationed at Home" follows a taxi driver anxious to catch a glimpse of the International Space Station during his overnight Christmas Eve shift in 1998. His plans get interrupted by a cast of eccentric strangers who, by night's end, help him understand his place in the world. The Ithaca College cinema production alum and 2015 Variety "110 Students to Watch" honoree has been working professionally as an editor since graduating. His film has premiered at Glasgow Film Festival, Buffalo International Film Festival, and Brooklyn Film Festival before returning home for the March 19 Cinemapolis screening.

stories.title: Vermont Paralympian Spencer Wood is going downhill, says he finds it uplifting
stories.summary: Spencer Wood, a 29-year-old Pittsfield skier who suffered a paralyzing stroke before birth, represented Vermont as its sole 2026 Paralympian. Born with cerebral palsy affecting the right side of his body, Wood competed on the world stage at the same Italian venues where Vermont-trained Olympians won medals weeks earlier.
stories.extended_summary: Spencer Wood suffered a stroke in the womb that left him paralyzed on his right side. As a child in Pittsfield, he wore a brace that looked like a ski boot while other kids ran around in sneakers. "I felt so different," he recalls. But the 29-year-old didn't let cerebral palsy stop him from becoming Vermont's sole 2026 Paralympian, competing in the same Italian venues where fellow Vermont-trained skiers like Mikaela Shiffrin and Jessie Diggins won Olympic medals weeks earlier. "What makes Paralympians just like Olympians is we're not letting anything limit us," Wood said. "Every single ounce of sweat that I put into the sport is the culmination of what I've learned and what I'm ready to show for it." He shares a birthday with his direct ancestor Benjamin Franklin — fitting for someone who refused to accept limitations.
stories.headline_split: Vermont Paralympian Spencer Wood is going downhill, says he finds it
fake_endings: the perfect time for quiet reflection
stories.fake_headline: Retired Teacher at 78 Completes All 50 State Marathons in Wheelchair

stories.title: Jadzia Axelrod Has Created the Trans Superhero We Always Needed
stories.summary: Transgender author Jadzia Axelrod created "Galaxy: The Prettiest Star," a 2022 graphic novel featuring a trans superhero, inspired by David Bowie's gender-defying artistry. The book she wished existed as a gender-dysphoric teenager won the Congressional Art Competition and offers representation she couldn't find growing up in rural North Carolina.
stories.extended_summary: The dedication page of "Galaxy: The Prettiest Star" reads: "For the girl who needed this book ages ago, and couldn't find it." Transgender author Jadzia Axelrod was writing to her teenage self — the one who grew up in small-town North Carolina finding solace in David Bowie's brazen gender ambiguity. When a high school friend gave her a mixtape of "Ziggy Stardust," she was blown away. "I had never heard music that was so unapologetically queer before," Axelrod said. Her 2022 graphic novel draws chapter titles from Bowie's 1973 "Aladdin Sane" album and features a transgender superhero — the representation she desperately needed but couldn't find. In high school, Axelrod's six-page comic won the Congressional Art Competition, the first comic to do so. Now she's giving other kids the hero she always needed.
stories.headline_split: Jadzia Axelrod Has Created the Trans Superhero We
fake_endings: Can Finally See on the Big Screen

stories.title: 26-03-18_D+C-The Good News
stories.summary: Filipino student Carvey Ehren Maigue developed AuREUS, a material that generates solar energy even on cloudy days by converting UV radiation into electricity. Made primarily from vegetable waste, it works longer than conventional panels since UV penetrates clouds. Meanwhile, a Nigerian monarch powers his palace with solar and distributes surplus electricity to the public.
stories.extended_summary: Cloudy days have always been solar energy's weakness — until now. Filipino student Carvey Ehren Maigue created AuREUS, a material that converts UV radiation into visible light and then electricity, meaning it generates power even when clouds block direct sunlight. Since UV radiation penetrates cloud cover, the technology works significantly longer than conventional solar panels and doesn't need precise sun alignment. The material is made mainly from vegetable waste. In Nigeria, the Olowu of Owu is putting solar innovation into practice differently: his palace runs entirely on solar energy, with surplus electricity distributed to power street lighting and health centers. He's also using 3D-printed cowry shell tokens tracked via cloud technology to ensure over 5,000 schoolchildren receive their meals.
stories.headline_split: 26-03-18_D+C-The Good

stories.title: TAKING A STAND
stories.summary: At 14, Derin Ege Sezgin spoke at the UN High-Level Political Forum, criticizing institutions for excluding young people from decision-making. The SDG Youth Connect founder later navigated age restrictions to participate in the UN General Assembly, advocating for youth voices in shaping global development goals they'll inherit.
stories.extended_summary: Most 14-year-olds don't get invited to United Nations events. Derin Ege Sezgin didn't just attend — he took the podium at the UN High-Level Political Forum in July 2025 and called out the institution for sidelining youth. Speaking for the Major Group for Children and Youth, he was blunt: young people get praised publicly but excluded procedurally. "We're the ones who are going to live with the consequences of whether these goals succeed or fail," he said. "Excluding us from shaping them just doesn't make sense." Two months later, he set his sights on the UN General Assembly's high-level week, but age restrictions nearly blocked him. As founder of SDG Youth Connect, Sezgin represents a generation demanding a seat at tables where their futures are being decided.

stories.title: Des Moines screenwriter Joshua Johnson on how a Hitchcock spoof he penned 10 years ago became Jason Biggs’ directorial debut
stories.summary: Iowa-raised screenwriter Joshua Johnson's decade-old Hitchcock spoof is now Jason Biggs' directorial debut. The thriller-comedy follows a man staging a home invasion to save his marriage—like Fargo minus the snow. Johnson now works in Hollywood from Des Moines, proving you don't have to leave home to make it big.
stories.extended_summary: A kid from West Des Moines used movies as a portal to escape the quiet suburbs—now he's the one making them. Joshua Johnson's first feature film, written with partner Jamie Napoli ten years ago, stars Jason Biggs in his directorial debut as a man who stages a fake home invasion to save his marriage (spoiler: it goes very wrong). Johnson moved back to Iowa while still working in the industry, dismantling the myth that serious creative careers require permanent relocation. The film blends Fargo-style dark comedy with relationship chaos, proof that Iowa imaginations can compete anywhere.

stories.title: Meli James Is Rewriting the Rules for Hawai‘i’s Entrepreneurs
stories.summary: Meli James is rewriting Hawaiʻi's entrepreneurial story. As co-founder of Mana Up and president of the Hawaiʻi Venture Capital Association, she's built the systems that let local businesses scale without fleeing to the mainland. Her work proves the Islands never lacked talent—just the support networks to make growth possible.
stories.extended_summary: For decades, ambitious Hawaiʻi entrepreneurs faced a brutal choice: stay small or leave home. Meli James spent the last ten years building a third option. As co-founder of Mana Up, she created the pathways, networks, and national visibility that local companies need to grow into durable enterprises without relocating. The challenge was never a shortage of talent or ideas, she explains—it was the missing infrastructure to help them scale. James meets me at The Pig and the Lady, a Vietnamese restaurant founded by a refugee family, the kind of business that embodies exactly what she's fighting for: personal storytelling meets exceptional craft, rooted firmly in the Islands.

stories.title: The art of College poetry
stories.summary: Harvard is home to three National Youth Poet Laureates—including current honoree Evan Wang '29, who's already performed everywhere from Google's London headquarters to the Smithsonian. His debut chapbook drops next month, and he's working with a composer on a choral piece premiering in June. He tests every line for how it sounds aloud, imagining hand gestures before he even writes it down.
stories.extended_summary: When Evan Wang sits down to write poetry—usually late at night in his dorm—he's already on stage. The Harvard first-year tests each line for how it will sound spoken aloud, planning which words to stress and what gestures to use. As the current National Youth Poet Laureate, he's in constant demand: performances at Google DeepMind in London, the Smithsonian, and the AFS Youth Assembly in New York. His debut chapbook 'Slow Burn' launches next month, and he's collaborating with a composer on a choral piece for the Mendelssohn Chorus of Philadelphia. Wang joins a lineage that includes Amanda Gorman and two other Harvard students who've held the title, proving poetry isn't just alive—it's thriving.

stories.summary: Peninsula College goalkeeper Max Woithe thought his pro soccer dreams ended in Germany—until he came to Washington state. Now the North Region's Defensive Player of the Year is heading back to sign with SC Preuβen Münster, a U-23 professional club. His childhood dream gets a contract on April 8.
stories.extended_summary: Max Woithe's professional soccer dreams stalled out in Germany as a kid—the kind of quiet death ambition dies when the path forward disappears. Then Peninsula College in Port Angeles offered him a spot, and everything changed. 'When I came here, I never thought it would be this good,' he says. 'If I never played here, I would never have this opportunity.' Now the North Region's Defensive Player of the Year is heading back to Münster, Germany, to sign a professional contract with SC Preuβen Münster's U-23 squad. He'll get a modest salary, housing, and the chance to keep studying while playing at a stadium with elite training facilities—everything he thought he'd never have.

stories.title: NBA Fan Travels The Country To Support Make-A-Wish
stories.summary: Justin Leusner is turning his love of basketball into something bigger: a tour of every NBA arena to raise money for Make-A-Wish. He's already raised over $45,000 toward his $100,000 goal, with just three arenas left before the fundraiser closes April 1st.
stories.extended_summary: Justin Leusner could have just bought season tickets. Instead, he's visiting every NBA arena in the country to raise money for Make-A-Wish—and Portland fans witnessed his mission Monday night when he showed up at their game. So far, he's raised over $45,000 of his $100,000 goal, using basketball as the vehicle to help critically ill kids get their wishes granted. 'I just want to use my love of basketball to bring a little joy to kids who really need it,' he said. With only three arenas left and the fundraiser closing April 1st, he's racing toward a finish line that means everything to families he'll never meet.

stories.title: Irvington, Sleepy Hollow Heads of the Class Named
stories.summary: Irvington's valedictorian Josh Greenbarg didn't just ace his classes—he founded Blazer Your Way, a nonprofit that gives blazers to young adults entering the workforce. He'll speak at graduation before heading to UPenn, having mastered Spanish, Latin, and Ancient Greek along the way. His secret? A million small choices.
stories.extended_summary: Josh Greenbarg became Irvington High's valedictorian through what he calls 'a million different little choices' made over four years—but his biggest achievement might be off the transcript. He founded Blazer Your Way, a nonprofit providing new or gently used blazers to young job seekers to boost their confidence and professionalism. Along the way, he fell in love with languages, expanding from Spanish to Latin and Ancient Greek, served as president of the Irvington Alumni Network, and played four years of varsity golf. Now he's heading to the University of Pennsylvania as a National Merit Scholar Finalist, ready to address his classmates at commencement about what hard work really looks like when you break it down.

stories.title: Inside a woman’s fight to free Nigerian detainees
stories.summary: Funke Adeoye's Hope Behind Bars Africa is fighting Nigeria's pre-trial detention crisis, where people sit in prison for months or years without conviction. Her organization expands legal representation and pushes systemic reform in a country where slow courts and limited legal aid trap thousands in limbo.
stories.extended_summary: More than three million people worldwide are locked up without conviction—and Nigeria sits at the sharp edge of this crisis. Funke Adeoye founded Hope Behind Bars Africa to fight a system where slow courts and weak legal aid leave people detained for months or years before they ever see trial. In some African countries, over 70% of prisoners haven't been convicted of anything; they're just stuck in a judicial backlog. Adeoye's organization works to expand legal representation, reduce unnecessary detention, and push for reforms that could free thousands trapped in pre-trial limbo. It's not glamorous work, but it's reshaping lives caught in a broken system that punishes people before proving they did anything wrong.

stories.title: Achieve Plant City Helps Individuals With Literacy Issues In The Community
stories.summary: Achieve Plant City founder Angelica Ibarra grew up with two illiterate parents, became a teen mom twice before 18, and lived in extreme poverty—then used education to break the cycle. Now her nonprofit helps local families do the same, offering literacy support that changes lives one reader at a time.
stories.extended_summary: Angelica Ibarra's parents couldn't read. She became a mother of two before turning 18 and lived in extreme poverty—until education gave her a way out. Now she runs Achieve Plant City, a nonprofit built on the belief that literacy is the great equalizer, offering tailored educational services to families facing the same barriers she once did. Her favorite success story? A mother whose young daughter struggled to read came for help; now the girl is thriving and the whole family's future looks different. Ibarra broke the cycle of illiteracy and poverty in her own life, and Achieve Plant City exists to give other families that same shot at transformation.
stories.headline_split: Achieve Plant City Helps Individuals With Literacy Issues

stories.title: Lighthouse Writers and MCA Denver Host Film Screening of Death by Numbers Ahead of Columbine 27th Anniversary
stories.summary: Sam Fuentes survived the Parkland shooting—wounded in her Holocaust Studies class while a classmate with an AR-15 killed 17 people. Now her Oscar-nominated documentary 'Death By Numbers' screens in Denver ahead of Columbine's 27th anniversary, weaving her poetry with her shooter's sentencing trial as she confronts what collective hate and restorative justice really mean.
stories.extended_summary: Sam Fuentes was shot in her Holocaust Studies class on Valentine's Day 2018, when a former student brought an AR-15 to Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School and killed 17 people. Now she's made an award-winning documentary about it—'Death By Numbers,' nominated for a 2025 Oscar—that Denver audiences will see March 27, just days before Columbine's 27th anniversary. The film isn't typical documentary work: it's an experiment interweaving Fuentes' poetry with footage from her shooter's sentencing trial, where she prepared to face the person who wounded her and murdered her classmates. She and her teacher examine brutal questions about collective hate and restorative justice in a society increasingly numb to gun violence and the cries of traumatized youth.

stories.title: A Billionaire-Backed Startup Wants to Grow 'Organ Sacks' to Replace Animal Testing
stories.summary: Bay Area biotech startup R3 Bio wants to replace animal testing with 'organ sacks'—structures containing all typical organs except a brain, rendering them unable to think or feel pain. The long-term goal? Human versions that could provide replacement organs for people who need them, embracing a 'replacement over repair' longevity strategy.
stories.extended_summary: As the Trump administration phases out animal testing, R3 Bio has a controversial pitch: grow nonsentient 'organ sacks' with everything but a brain. No thoughts, no pain, no ethical dilemma—at least that's the argument. The Bay Area startup is currently aiming to make monkey versions for drug testing, backed by Singapore's Immortal Dragons longevity fund, which believes replacement beats repair for treating disease and aging. The real goal, though, is human organ sacks—headless 'bodyoids' that could provide organs on demand. It's straight out of science fiction, raising questions about ethics, humanity, and what exactly we're willing to grow in a lab to save lives or extend them.

stories.title: Harvey Park Is Getting a New Farmers’ Market This Summer
stories.summary: Harvey Park residents are getting their own farmers' market this May, after Allie Bronston spent two years building what she calls a "one-stop grocery shop" featuring over 50 vendors. The new market at Kunsmiller Creative Arts Academy will bring fresh, local food to a densely populated Denver neighborhood that's been surprisingly underserved despite having urban farms nearby.
stories.extended_summary: Instead of just shopping at a different store, Allie Bronston created an entire farmers' market from scratch. After nearly two years of planning, the Harvey Park Farmers Market debuts May 2 in southwest Denver — a neighborhood that's the city's most populated zip code but has been oddly cut off from fresh, local food despite nearby urban farms. Bronston and her husband recruited over 50 vendors based on a typical grocery list, aiming to replace trips to Kroger or Whole Foods entirely. The market will set up in the Kunsmiller Creative Arts Academy parking lot, offering everything from produce to specialty items in one community-focused stop.

stories.title: Living Water Connections Turns Comics Into Literacy Tool for Kids
stories.summary: After his son Adin spent difficult days hospitalized with Crohn's disease, Justin Behrens helped turn the teen's vision into reality: a comic newspaper created by famous artists and delivered to kids in hospitals. That project has grown into Living Water Connections, a State College nonprofit using comics and graphic novels to bring joy and literacy to young readers facing hard times.
stories.extended_summary: Adin Behrens was stuck in a hospital bed with Crohn's disease, feeling like there was nothing for him there. So he imagined something better: a comic newspaper made by famous artists that could give other kids something exciting to read during long stays and treatments. His father Justin helped make it real, recruiting comic creators to contribute strips and articles. What started as one family's response to illness has become Living Water Connections, a literacy-driven nonprofit in State College that now delivers quarterly comic newspapers and runs programs showing how graphic novels can be both entertainment and a meaningful tool for young readers going through their own difficult moments.

stories.title: Single? These New Yorkers want you to date their friends.
stories.summary: Tired of watching her single guy friends strike out at bars, married music photographer Brit O'Brien created "Date My Friend" — an event where wingmen pitch their besties to crowds of singles. The LA-based series sold out its first six events and is expanding to New York, where March dates at Night Club 101 and Cassette also sold out fast.
stories.extended_summary: Brit O'Brien kept noticing the same thing: her single guy friends would go to shows and never talk to anyone. As a married music photographer with no dating stakes herself, she decided to fix it by flipping the script entirely. At "Date My Friend" events, wingmen take center stage to sell their single besties to crowds of eager bachelors and bachelorettes — creating a space that feels like a concert venue but where people actually connect. The first event in LA this January unexpectedly went viral on Partiful ("we didn't even know that was a thing that could happen"), then sold out, along with the next five. Now the series is landing in New York with the same result: instant sellouts showing just how hungry people are for a fresh take on meeting someone.
stories.headline_split: Single? These New Yorkers want you to

stories.title: In L.A., Christina Quarles Confronts the Tension Between Body, Space and Identity
stories.summary: Fresh from Yale, artist Christina Quarles rose quickly in the contemporary art world with paintings that challenge fixed ideas of body, identity and space. Her latest work at Hauser & Wirth Los Angeles explores even deeper instability, drawing from her own recent experiences of displacement as a queer, mixed-race woman navigating a fluid sense of self.
stories.extended_summary: Christina Quarles' interlocking human figures refuse to behave — they defy anatomy, scale, and perspective, exploring how identities resist categories and bodies resist traditional shapes. Since graduating from Yale, the queer, mixed-race artist has established herself internationally, investigating not just gender and identity politics but the technological forces eroding any unified sense of presence in space. Her latest body of work at Hauser & Wirth in Los Angeles goes further into instability and ambiguity, with theatrically distorted figures that reflect the profound displacement she's experienced in recent years. At the core is the timeless negotiation between inner and external worlds, captured in paint that refuses to settle.

stories.title: From hay field to full-diet farm: Rainshadow Organics grows a community in Central Oregon
stories.summary: What started as a family hay field in Central Oregon has become Rainshadow Organics, an 80-acre farm that provides nearly everything 70 families eat through a "full-diet" CSA program. Sarahlee Lawrence, who studied environmental science and worked as a Grand Canyon river guide, returned home to create an ambitious experiment in local food after reading Michael Pollan's work on agriculture's environmental impact.
stories.extended_summary: Sarahlee Lawrence spent years guiding river trips in the Grand Canyon and Peru, her career focused on conservation. But reading an essay by Michael Pollan sparked a realization: "the food we eat is our biggest environmental impact." She returned to her family's 80-acre hay operation in Central Oregon in the late 2000s and asked her parents for permission to try something radically different. Today, that land is Rainshadow Organics, an organic farm producing vegetables, grains, dairy, eggs and meat — essentially everything a family needs to eat — for roughly 70 households through a "full-diet" CSA program. Around 180 more families participate in seasonal vegetable boxes, and the farm supplies local restaurants and markets, proving an ambitious experiment in local food can work.
stories.headline_split: From hay field to full-diet farm: Rainshadow Organics grows

stories.title: Keya Tama, famous for murals across the globe, brings canvas and tapestry works to Tampa this weekend
stories.summary: Artist Keya Tama, now 28, was born into graffiti culture in Cape Town and raised by two teenage street artists — muralist Faith XLVII and tattooist Tyler B. Murphy. Growing up painting alongside his parents, he's become known for murals worldwide and is bringing canvas and tapestry works to Tampa this weekend.
stories.extended_summary: Most artists go to art school. Keya Tama was born into paint jams and skate parks. The 28-year-old grew up in Cape Town, South Africa, raised by two teenage graffiti writers — his muralist mother Faith XLVII and his fine-artist-turned-tattooist father Tyler B. Murphy. He learned by doing, painting alongside his parents from childhood in a culture where street art wasn't just a hobby but a way of life. Now internationally recognized for his murals across the globe, Tama is bringing a different side of his practice to Tampa this weekend: canvas and tapestry works that translate his street-born aesthetic into gallery form.

stories.title: Linzi Murray and her West Des Moines bookstore/cafe are hitting their stride
stories.summary: Linzi Murray, who was adopted from China and grew up finding refuge in libraries while her alcoholic father struggled, now runs Reading in Public Bookstore and Cafe in West Des Moines. Guided by her late father's advice to "follow joy," she's created the kind of warm, inclusive space that gave her comfort as a child — complete with thoughtful curation and community events.
stories.extended_summary: When Linzi Murray's father Keith would binge-drink on weekends, her mom would take her to libraries and bookstores — safe spaces where an adopted Chinese girl navigating mostly white Kansas City could feel less alone. Keith died of lung cancer 10 years ago, but not before giving his daughter lasting advice: "As long as you follow joy, you'll never go wrong in your life." Those words became her first tattoo and the philosophy behind Reading in Public Bookstore and Cafe, now three years old in West Des Moines. Murray, entering her 30s, has built exactly the kind of refuge she needed as a kid: warm paint colors, inclusive romance and sci-fi, social justice books for children, and evening events that help patrons feel understood without saying a word.
stories.headline_split: Linzi Murray and her West Des Moines bookstore/cafe are

stories.title: New cultural graduation celebration is coming to The Beach
stories.summary: Long Beach State's Middle Eastern North African student group is launching the university's first MENA cultural graduation ceremony this April. Led by students Danya Ali and Lavinia Badrous, the event will feature cultural dance, music, custom sashes and correctly pronounced names — creating a space where students can celebrate their heritage while receiving diplomas.
stories.extended_summary: At Long Beach State's spring commencement this April 18, Middle Eastern and North African students will finally see themselves reflected. The student-led MENA group is bringing the university's first cultural graduation to The Pointe, where graduates can walk the stage in custom sashes, hear their names pronounced correctly, and immerse themselves in music and dance from their own heritage. Secretary Danya Ali, a third-year psychology student graduating early, says it's about more than ceremony — it's about "being able to look someone in the eye and feel understood without even sharing a conversation." Founder Lavinia Badrous spearheaded the effort with minimal administrative help, creating space for students from North Africa and Southwest Asia to claim representation they've always deserved.

stories.fake_headline: Annual Lantern Festival Returns to Riverside Park After Three-Year Pause

stories.title: PHOTOS: Queer Asian Museum opens in St. Petersburg
stories.summary: SilkPride opened the world's first Queer Asian Museum in St. Petersburg's EDGE District on March 21, creating a dedicated space for queer Asian identity, culture and diaspora through art and history. Founder Leo Andersen says the museum activates stories that are "not new, not temporary and not invisible," building what he calls a "micro-ecosystem" where those narratives can be shared and expanded.
stories.extended_summary: Leo Andersen welcomed supporters to the opening of something that's never existed before: the world's first museum dedicated to queer Asian identity, culture and diaspora. The Queer Asian Museum, opened March 21 in St. Petersburg's EDGE District, is about "visibility, audibility and possibility" — a micro-ecosystem where queer Asian stories aren't just displayed but activated and expanded in a city Andersen already calls home. City LGBTQ+ Liaison Nathan Bruemmer, presenting Andersen with St. Pete's Pride flag, emphasized the stakes: "This museum reminds us that queer Asian stories are not new, not temporary and not invisible. They're part of the fabric of our communities... preserving these stories ensures that future generations will know that progress did not happen by accident."

stories.fake_headline: PHOTOS: Indigenous Women's Art Gallery Debuts in Minneapolis With 75 Artists

stories.title: Enchanted Creators Collective works its magic in Visalia
stories.summary: Ashley McIntosh's Enchanted Creators Collective has transformed Visalia's event scene, growing from a 10-vendor gathering to large-scale themed events drawing thousands from across California. The group's next free event, a Fairy Door Hunt and Picnic on April 26, will turn Mooney Grove Park into an immersive storybook world with vendor markets, interactive craft stations and a Tinker Bell cosplayer.
stories.extended_summary: The Central Valley didn't know it needed a Renaissance fair for fairies — until Ashley McIntosh created one. Her Enchanted Creators Collective has spent years building something unexpected: large-scale, themed events that bring together small businesses, artists, cosplayers, bakers and performers under one magical umbrella. What started as a 10-vendor gathering now draws thousands of attendees from across California and beyond. McIntosh's 3rd annual Fairy Door Hunt and Picnic on April 26 will transform Mooney Grove Park into an immersive, nature-inspired setting with about 30 fairy-aesthetic vendors, craft stations for pixie dust necklaces, a wishing well, bubbles, and a Tinker Bell ready for photos. The best part? It's completely free, proving community magic doesn't need a price tag.
